content:
How we built a serverless, cache-first platform that handled 29k page views with zero backend servers.

8 min read
1 hour ago

--

A few days ago, I wrote about how LabXam started from a simple frustration before my lab exams and eventually grew into a platform used by thousands of students during exam season.
That article was mostly about the journey.
You can read it here: How LabXam Happened
This one is about the engineering.
Over the last few months, LabXam has gone through multiple iterations. It started as a weekend project, migrated from Firebase to Supabase, gained an admin dashboard, real-time updates, AI-generated solutions, rate limiting, caching and quite a few bugs that taught me more than any tutorial ever could.
None of these decisions were made because they were the вҖңbestвҖқ technologies. Most of them were made because they solved a problem that existed at that moment.
In this article, IвҖҷll walk through the architecture behind LabXam, the trade-offs I made, the mistakes that forced me to rethink parts of the system and what IвҖҷd build differently if I were starting from scratch today.
Defining the Problem
At its core, LabXam is a content management system for previous year lab examination questions.
Students only see a simple interface where they select their semester, subject and evaluation type to browse questions. Behind that interface, however, the application has to manage structured data, handle user submissions, enforce access control and keep everything synchronized in real time.
When I started building the project, I wanted to keep the experience as frictionless as possible. A student preparing for an exam shouldnвҖҷt have to create an account or navigate through multiple pages just to find a question. Every unnecessary click was another reason for someone to close the website and go back to searching through WhatsApp groups.
That led to a few design principles that shaped the rest of the application:

вҖў Browsing questions should require no authentication.

вҖў Adding and managing questions should be restricted to administrators.

вҖў New questions should appear to users without requiring manual updates.

вҖў The architecture should remain simple enough for a small team to maintain.
With these goals in mind, I started designing the overall architecture.
High Level Architecture
One of the earliest decisions I made was to avoid building a traditional backend server.
Since LabXam is primarily a read-heavy application, introducing an Express or Spring Boot server would have added another layer to maintain without providing much value. Instead, I chose a client-first architecture where the React application communicates directly with Supabase.
The overall architecture looks like this:

Supabase в”Ӯ в–ј
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Store Solution
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в–ј в–ј в–ј в–ј
RLS Authentication Realtime Return ResultMost users simply read data. Questions rarely change, but they are viewed thousands of times during examination periods. By letting the frontend query Supabase directly, I eliminated an entire backend layer while still benefiting from authentication, Row Level Security (RLS), real-time subscriptions and PostgreSQL.
Of course, this doesnвҖҷt mean there is вҖңno backend.вҖқ Supabase handles authentication, authorization, database access and real-time communication behind the scenes. From the applicationвҖҷs perspective, it acts as a Backend-as-a-Service, allowing me to focus on the product instead of maintaining API endpoints.
As the project grew, additional features like AI-generated solutions, feedback collection, question submissions and admin workflows were built on top of this same architecture without requiring any major redesign.
This architecture wasnвҖҷt chosen because it was the most sophisticated. It was chosen because it solved the projectвҖҷs requirements with the least complexity. For a read-heavy application maintained by a two-person team, removing the need for a dedicated backend significantly reduced operational overhead while still providing authentication, security and realtime capabilities through Supabase.
Designing the Database
The database is centered around a single questions table. It stores:

вҖў semester

вҖў subject

вҖў year

вҖў evaluation_type

вҖў section

вҖў question
All core features read from or write to this table.
Supporting Tables

вҖў pending
Stores user-submitted questions before admin approval.

вҖў reports
Stores reports for incorrect or outdated questions.

вҖў feedback
Stores user feedback and ratings.

вҖў ai_solutions
Caches AI-generated solutions for questions.

вҖў activity_log
Tracks admin actions (create, update, delete).

вҖў page_views
Tracks anonymous visits for analytics.

вҖў admins
Stores authorized admin users.
Row Level Security (RLS) Policies
RLS is enabled on all tables.
Public Read Access

вҖў questions: SELECT allowed for all users

вҖў ai_solutions: SELECT allowed for all users
Restricted Write Access (Admins Only)

вҖў questions: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE allowed only if user exists in admins

вҖў pending: DELETE allowed only for admins (after approval)

вҖў reports: DELETE allowed only for admins

вҖў feedback: DELETE allowed only for admins

вҖў activity_log: INSERT allowed only for admins
Public Write Access

вҖў pending: INSERT allowed for all users

вҖў reports: INSERT allowed for all users

вҖў feedback: INSERT allowed for all users

вҖў page_views: INSERT allowed for all users
Admin Validation Policy

вҖў All admin-restricted operations check if auth.uid() exists in admins table
This setup keeps read operations open while restricting all critical modifications to authorized admins only.
AI Solution Generation
One of the most requested features after the first release was the ability to generate solutions for lab questions.
The straightforward approach would have been to call an LLM every time a student clicked the вҖңGenerate SolutionвҖқ button. While simple to implement, it would also be slow, expensive and wasteful. During examination periods, dozens of students often request solutions for the same questions.
Instead, I implemented a cache-first approach.
When a user requests a solution, the application first checks the ai_solutions table to see if one already exists. If it does, the cached solution is returned immediately. Otherwise, a request is sent to the NVIDIA API, the generated solution is stored in the database and the response is returned to the user.
Without caching, the number of API calls could grow indefinitely since every solution request would trigger a new generation. With the cache-first approach, the theoretical upper bound is the number of questions in the questions table, as each question's solution is generated only once and reused for all subsequent requests.

Return SolutionThis approach turned out to be one of the biggest performance improvements in the application. Most solution requests during peak traffic are now served directly from the cache instead of triggering a new AI generation.
Community Question Submission Workflow
One challenge with LabXam is that new questions have to be collected every semester. Allowing anyone to write directly to the questions table would have made the database vulnerable to spam, duplicate entries and incorrect data.
Instead, user submissions follow a simple moderation pipeline.
When a student submits a question through the /submit page, it is stored in the pending table instead of being published immediately. Administrators can then review the submission, verify its correctness, and either approve or reject it.
Approved questions are moved to the questions table and become instantly available to every user through Supabase Realtime. This keeps the main dataset reliable while still allowing the community to contribute new content.
User

questions RemovedSeparating user submissions from production data keeps the review process simple and prevents unverified content from appearing on the platform.
Feedback Pipeline
After the initial launch, we added a simple feedback system to understand how students were using the platform and what needed improvement. Users could rate their experience and optionally leave suggestions, all of which were stored in the feedback table.
Rather than treating feedback as just another feature, we used it to guide development. Several improvements, including AI-generated solutions and displaying exam dates instead of just years, came directly from recurring suggestions submitted by students.
Issue Reporting Pipeline
As the number of questions grew, we needed a simple way for users to report incorrect or outdated content. Instead of allowing direct edits, every report is stored in the reports table with the relevant question details.
Administrators can review these reports from the dashboard and take appropriate action, such as correcting or removing the question. This keeps the dataset accurate while preventing unauthorized modifications to production data.
Optimization
LabXam operates under a read-heavy workload, especially during examination periods where the same question sets are requested repeatedly within short intervals. To handle this efficiently, several optimizations were implemented to reduce database load and improve response times.
A second layer of caching was introduced for the questions table. Instead of querying PostgreSQL on every request, frequently accessed question sets are cached and served directly. Since updates to questions are relatively infrequent, this cache remains valid for most requests and significantly reduces redundant database reads.
Query patterns were also simplified to avoid unnecessary joins and heavy computations at runtime. Data required for rendering was structured to be fetched in minimal queries, reducing latency and improving consistency under load.
Additionally, the system avoids real-time recomputation wherever possible. AI-generated solutions are computed once and stored, ensuring that repeated requests do not trigger expensive operations.
These optimizations proved particularly valuable during examination periods, where LabXam handled over 7,000+ visits and 29,000+ page views while continuing to serve requests with minimal latency.
Why We Migrated from Firebase to Supabase
Firebase became difficult to manage as the project grew. Working with nested JSON made inserting, updating and organizing questions cumbersome and we faced occasional data duplication issues.
Since we were learning SQL at the time, moving to Supabase (PostgreSQL) felt natural. A relational database made data easier to query, maintain and manage.
It also simplified admin workflows вҖ” adding, editing and reviewing questions became much more straightforward. Additionally, SupabaseвҖҷs Row Level Security (RLS) provided better control over data access without needing a custom authorization system.
Why We DidnвҖҷt Care About SEO
YouвҖҷll notice there isnвҖҷt much SEO work in LabXam. ThatвҖҷs intentional.
The platform was primarily built for our college, so ranking on Google was not exactly a priority. Of course, anyone can use it if they find it, but we were not exactly optimizing for global domination. рҹҳ„
CI/CD
Every change to LabXam goes through a CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions and Vercel. WeвҖҷve protected the main branch, so neither of us can push directly. This became especially important during exam season when breaking production was not an option. Every change must go through a pull request, pass all tes
